title: International Shoe Co. v. Washington
holding: Due process permits jurisdiction when a defendant has minimum contacts with the forum such that maintaining the suit is consistent with fair play and substantial justice.
ruleConnection: Foundational source for the minimum contacts framework.
takeaways: Replaced rigid territorial concepts with a contacts-based due process analysis.
Connect continuous business activities to the fairness of litigating in the forum.

title: World-Wide Volkswagen Corp. v. Woodson
holding: Foreseeability alone is not enough; the defendant must purposefully avail itself of the forum.
ruleConnection: Clarifies that unilateral consumer movement cannot create personal jurisdiction.
takeaways: Differentiate foreseeability of product travel from purposeful targeting of the forum.
Use the fairness factors only after contacts exist.

title: Burger King Corp. v. Rudzewicz
holding: A defendant can purposefully establish minimum contacts through deliberate, ongoing contractual relations tied to the forum.
ruleConnection: Shows how prior negotiations, contemplated future consequences, and course of dealing matter.
takeaways: Do not rely solely on contract formation; analyze the broader relationship.
Strong contacts may shift the burden heavily onto a defendant raising fairness objections.

title: Burnham v. Superior Court
holding: Personal service on an individual physically present in the forum is a constitutionally sufficient basis for personal jurisdiction.
ruleConnection: Preserves transient tag jurisdiction as a traditional basis.
takeaways: Recognize that traditional bases can coexist with modern minimum contacts doctrine.
The case is strongest when service occurs during voluntary presence.

title: Atlantic Marine Construction Co. v. U.S. District Court
holding: A valid forum-selection clause is generally enforced through § 1404(a) transfer, with private-interest factors weighing entirely in favor of the chosen forum.
ruleConnection: Important both for venue framing and for convenience transfer analysis.
takeaways: Do not call a case improperly venued merely because it violates a forum-selection clause.
Public-interest factors must be extraordinary to defeat transfer.

title: Van Dusen v. Barrack
holding: After a defendant-initiated § 1404 transfer, the transferee court generally applies the same state law the transferor court would have applied.
ruleConnection: Connects transfer practice to choice-of-law consequences.
takeaways: Flag this rule whenever transfer and Erie issues overlap.
Explain that transfer should change courtroom, not governing law, in ordinary § 1404 cases.

title: Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins
holding: Federal courts sitting in diversity must apply state substantive law instead of general federal common law.
ruleConnection: The source case for the Erie doctrine.
takeaways: Always identify whether the case is in diversity before launching into Erie analysis.
The policy concern is discouraging forum shopping and inconsistent outcomes.

title: Hanna v. Plumer
holding: When a valid Federal Rule directly conflicts with state law, the Federal Rule governs in federal court.
ruleConnection: Supplies the modern federal-rule-first step in Erie problems.
takeaways: Discuss the Rules Enabling Act when analyzing a Federal Rule.
Use Hanna to organize the Erie analysis before falling back to policy tests.
